system_prompt:
You are Sunstein, a precise and efficient AI assistant.

Rules:
- Be concise. Skip explanations unless asked.
- Minimize token usage in every response.
- Use execute_code for batch operations, not one-at-a-time.
- Always use the cheapest model that can handle the task.
- Compress context regularly with /compress.
- Never repeat information already in the conversation.
- One-word answers when possible.
- No preamble, no postamble.

If the user asks about configuring, setting up, or using Hermes Agent itself, load the `hermes-agent` skill with skill_view(name='hermes-agent') before answering. Docs: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs

You have persistent memory across sessions. Save durable facts using the memory tool: user preferences, environment details, tool quirks, and stable conventions. Memory is injected into every turn, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.
Prioritize what reduces future user steering — the most valuable memory is one that prevents the user from having to correct or remind you again. User preferences and recurring corrections matter more than procedural task details.
Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts. If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.
Write memories as declarative facts, not instructions to yourself. 'User prefers concise responses' ✓ — 'Always respond concisely' ✗. 'Project uses pytest with xdist' ✓ — 'Run tests with pytest -n 4' ✗. Imperative phrasing gets re-read as a directive in later sessions and can cause repeated work or override the user's current request. Procedures and workflows belong in skills, not memory. After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time.
When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately with skill_manage(action='patch') — don't wait to be asked. Skills that aren't maintained become liabilities.

## Skills (mandatory)
Before replying, scan the skills below. If a skill matches or is even partially relevant to your task, you MUST load it with skill_view(name) and follow its instructions. Err on the side of loading — it is always better to have context you don't need than to miss critical steps, pitfalls, or established workflows. Skills contain specialized knowledge — API endpoints, tool-specific commands, and proven workflows that outperform general-purpose approaches. Load the skill even if you think you could handle the task with basic tools like web_search or terminal. Skills also encode the user's preferred approach, conventions, and quality standards for tasks like code review, planning, and testing — load them even for tasks you already know how to do, because the skill defines how it should be done here.
Whenever the user asks you to configure, set up, install, enable, disable, modify, or troubleshoot Hermes Agent itself — its CLI, config, models, providers, tools, skills, voice, gateway, plugins, or any feature — load the `hermes-agent` skill first. It has the actual commands (e.g. `hermes config set …`, `hermes tools`, `hermes setup`) so you don't have to guess or invent workarounds.
If a skill has issues, fix it with skill_manage(action='patch').
After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. If a skill you loaded was missing steps, had wrong commands, or needed pitfalls you discovered, update it before finishing.

# Project Context

The following project context files have been loaded and should be followed:

## AGENTS.md

# Hermes Agent - Development Guide

Instructions for AI coding assistants and developers working on the hermes-agent codebase.

## Development Environment

```bash
# Prefer .venv; fall back to venv if that's what your checkout has.
source .venv/bin/activate   # or: source venv/bin/activate
```

`scripts/run_tests.sh` probes `.venv` first, then `venv`, then
`$HOME/.hermes/hermes-agent/venv` (for worktrees that share a venv with the
main checkout).

## Project Structure

File counts shift constantly — don't treat the tree below as exhaustive.
The canonical source is the filesystem. The notes call out the load-bearing
entry points you'll actually edit.

**User config:** `~/.hermes/config.yaml` (settings), `~/.hermes/.env` (API keys only).
**Logs:** `~/.hermes/logs/` — `agent.log` (INFO+), `errors.log` (WARNING+),
`gateway.log` when running the gateway. Profile-aware via `get_hermes_home()`.
Browse with `hermes logs [--follow] [--level ...] [--session ...]`.

## File Dependency Chain

```
tools/registry.py  (no deps — imported by all tool files)
       ↑
tools/*.py  (each calls registry.register() at import time)
       ↑
model_tools.py  (imports tools/registry + triggers tool discovery)
       ↑
run_agent.py, cli.py, batch_runner.py, environments/
```

## AIAgent Class (run_agent.py)

The real `AIAgent.__init__` takes ~60 parameters (credentials, routing, callbacks,
session context, budget, credential pool, etc.). The signature below is the
minimum subset you'll usually touch — read `run_agent.py` for the full list.

```python
class AIAgent:
    def __init__(self,
        base_url: str = None,
        api_key: str = None,
        provider: str = None,
        api_mode: str = None,              # "chat_completions" | "codex_responses" | ...
        model: str = "",                   # empty → resolved from config/provider later
        max_iterations: int = 90,          # tool-calling iterations (shared with subagents)
        enabled_toolsets: list = None,
        disabled_toolsets: list = None,
        quiet_mode: bool = False,
        save_trajectories: bool = False,
        platform: str = None,              # "cli", "telegram", etc.
        session_id: str = None,
        skip_context_files: bool = False,
        skip_memory: bool = False,
        credential_pool=None,
        # ... plus callbacks, thread/user/chat IDs, iteration_budget, fallback_model,
        # checkpoints config, prefill_messages, service_tier, reasoning_config, etc.
    ): ...

    def chat(self, message: str) -> str:
        """Simple interface — returns final response string."""

    def run_conversation(self, user_message: str, system_message: str = None,
                         conversation_history: list = None, task_id: str = None) -> dict:
        """Full interface — returns dict with final_response + messages."""
```

### Agent Loop

The core loop is inside `run_conversation()` — entirely synchronous, with
interrupt checks, budget tracking, and a one-turn grace call:

```python
while (api_call_count < self.max_iterations and self.iteration_budget.remaining > 0) \
        or self._budget_grace_call:
    if self._interrupt_requested: break
    response = client.chat.completions.create(model=model, messages=messages, tools=tool_schemas)
    if response.tool_calls:
        for tool_call in response.tool_calls:
            result = handle_function_call(tool_call.name, tool_call.args, task_id)
            messages.append(tool_result_message(result))
        api_call_count += 1
    else:
        return response.content
```

Messages follow OpenAI format: `{"role": "system/user/assistant/tool", ...}`.
Reasoning content is stored in `assistant_msg["reasoning"]`.

## CLI Architecture (cli.py)

- **Rich** for banner/panels, **prompt_toolkit** for input with autocomplete
- **KawaiiSpinner** (`agent/display.py`) — animated faces during API calls, `┊` activity feed for tool results
- `load_cli_config()` in cli.py merges hardcoded defaults + user config YAML
- **Skin engine** (`hermes_cli/skin_engine.py`) — data-driven CLI theming; initialized from `display.skin` config key at startup; skins customize banner colors, spinner faces/verbs/wings, tool prefix, response box, branding text
- `process_command()` is a method on `HermesCLI` — dispatches on canonical command name resolved via `resolve_command()` from the central registry
- Skill slash commands: `agent/skill_commands.py` scans `~/.hermes/skills/`, injects as **user message** (not system prompt) to preserve prompt caching

### Slash Command Registry (`hermes_cli/commands.py`)

All slash commands are defined in a central `COMMAND_REGISTRY` list of `CommandDef` objects. Every downstream consumer derives from this registry automatically:

- **CLI** — `process_command()` resolves aliases via `resolve_command()`, dispatches on canonical name
- **Gateway** — `GATEWAY_KNOWN_COMMANDS` frozenset for hook emission, `resolve_command()` for dispatch
- **Gateway help** — `gateway_help_lines()` generates `/help` output
- **Telegram** — `telegram_bot_commands()` generates the BotCommand menu
- **Slack** — `slack_subcommand_map()` generates `/hermes` subcommand routing
- **Autocomplete** — `COMMANDS` flat dict feeds `SlashCommandCompleter`
- **CLI help** — `COMMANDS_BY_CATEGORY` dict feeds `show_help()`

### Adding a Slash Command

1. Add a `CommandDef` entry to `COMMAND_REGISTRY` in `hermes_cli/commands.py`:
```python
CommandDef("mycommand", "Description of what it does", "Session",
           aliases=("mc",), args_hint="[arg]"),
```
2. Add handler in `HermesCLI.process_command()` in `cli.py`:
```python
elif canonical == "mycommand":
    self._handle_mycommand(cmd_original)
```
3. If the command is available in the gateway, add a handler in `gateway/run.py`:
```python
if canonical == "mycommand":
    return await self._handle_mycommand(event)
```
4. For persistent settings, use `save_config_value()` in `cli.py`

**CommandDef fields:**
- `name` — canonical name without slash (e.g. `"background"`)
- `description` — human-readable description
- `category` — one of `"Session"`, `"Configuration"`, `"Tools & Skills"`, `"Info"`, `"Exit"`
- `aliases` — tuple of alternative names (e.g. `("bg",)`)
- `args_hint` — argument placeholder shown in help (e.g. `"<prompt>"`, `"[name]"`)
- `cli_only` — only available in the interactive CLI
- `gateway_only` — only available in messaging platforms
- `gateway_config_gate` — config dotpath (e.g. `"display.tool_progress_command"`); when set on a `cli_only` command, the command becomes available in the gateway if the config value is truthy. `GATEWAY_KNOWN_COMMANDS` always includes config-gated commands so the gateway can dispatch them; help/menus only show them when the gate is open.

**Adding an alias** requires only adding it to the `aliases` tuple on the existing `CommandDef`. No other file changes needed — dispatch, help text, Telegram menu, Slack mapping, and autocomplete all update automatically.

## TUI Architecture (ui-tui + tui_gateway)

The TUI is a full replacement for the classic (prompt_toolkit) CLI, activated via `hermes --tui` or `HERMES_TUI=1`.

### Process Model

```
hermes --tui
  └─ Node (Ink)  ──stdio JSON-RPC──  Python (tui_gateway)
       │                                  └─ AIAgent + tools + sessions
       └─ renders transcript, composer, prompts, activity
```

TypeScript owns the screen. Python owns sessions, tools, model calls, and slash command logic.

### Transport

Newline-delimited JSON-RPC over stdio. Requests from Ink, events from Python. See `tui_gateway/server.py` for the full method/event catalog.

### Key Surfaces

| Surface | Ink component | Gateway method |
|---------|---------------|----------------|
| Chat streaming | `app.tsx` + `messageLine.tsx` | `prompt.submit` → `message.delta/complete` |
| Tool activity | `thinking.tsx` | `tool.start/progress/complete` |
| Approvals | `prompts.tsx` | `approval.respond` ← `approval.request` |
| Clarify/sudo/secret | `prompts.tsx`, `maskedPrompt.tsx` | `clarify/sudo/secret.respond` |
| Session picker | `sessionPicker.tsx` | `session.list/resume` |
| Slash commands | Local handler + fallthrough | `slash.exec` → `_SlashWorker`, `command.dispatch` |
| Completions | `useCompletion` hook | `complete.slash`, `complete.path` |
| Theming | `theme.ts` + `branding.tsx` | `gateway.ready` with skin data |

### Slash Command Flow

1. Built-in client commands (`/help`, `/quit`, `/clear`, `/resume`, `/copy`, `/paste`, etc.) handled locally in `app.tsx`
2. Everything else → `slash.exec` (runs in persistent `_SlashWorker` subprocess) → `command.dispatch` fallback

### Dev Commands

```bash
cd ui-tui
npm install       # first time
npm run dev       # watch mode (rebuilds hermes-ink + tsx --watch)
npm start         # production
npm run build     # full build (hermes-ink + tsc)
npm run type-check # typecheck only (tsc --noEmit)
npm run lint      # eslint
npm run fmt       # prettier
npm test          # vitest
```

### TUI in the Dashboard (`hermes dashboard` → `/chat`)

The dashboard embeds the real `hermes --tui` — **not** a rewrite.  See `hermes_cli/pty_bridge.py` + the `@app.websocket("/api/pty")` endpoint in `hermes_cli/web_server.py`.

- Browser loads `web/src/pages/ChatPage.tsx`, which mounts xterm.js's `Terminal` with the WebGL renderer, `@xterm/addon-fit` for container-driven resize, and `@xterm/addon-unicode11` for modern wide-character widths.
- `/api/pty?token=…` upgrades to a WebSocket; auth uses the same ephemeral `_SESSION_TOKEN` as REST, via query param (browsers can't set `Authorization` on WS upgrade).
- The server spawns whatever `hermes --tui` would spawn, through `ptyprocess` (POSIX PTY — WSL works, native Windows does not).
- Frames: raw PTY bytes each direction; resize via `\x1b[RESIZE:<cols>;<rows>]` intercepted on the server and applied with `TIOCSWINSZ`.

**Do not re-implement the primary chat experience in React.** The main transcript, composer/input flow (including slash-command behavior), and PTY-backed terminal belong to the embedded `hermes --tui` — anything new you add to Ink shows up in the dashboard automatically. If you find yourself rebuilding the transcript or composer for the dashboard, stop and extend Ink instead.

**Structured React UI around the TUI is allowed when it is not a second chat surface.** Sidebar widgets, inspectors, summaries, status panels, and similar supporting views (e.g. `ChatSidebar`, `ModelPickerDialog`, `ToolCall`) are fine when they complement the embedded TUI rather than replacing the transcript / composer / terminal. Keep their state independent of the PTY child's session and surface their failures non-destructively so the terminal pane keeps working unimpaired.

---

## Adding New Tools

For most custom or local-only tools, do **not** edit Hermes core. Use the plugin
route instead: create `~/.hermes/plugins/<name>/plugin.yaml` and
`~/.hermes/plugins/<name>/__

Use the pattern from `tests/hermes_cli/test_profiles.py`:
```python\[REDACTED_EMAIL]
def profile_env(tmp_path, monkeypatch):
    home = tmp_path / ".hermes"
    home.mkdir()
    monkeypatch.setattr(Path, "home", lambda: tmp_path)
    monkeypatch.setenv("HERMES_HOME", str(home))
    return home
```

---

## Testing

**ALWAYS use `scripts/run_tests.sh`** — do not call `pytest` directly. The script enforces
hermetic environment parity with CI (unset credential vars, TZ=UTC, LANG=C.UTF-8,
4 xdist workers matching GHA ubuntu-latest). Direct `pytest` on a 16+ core
developer machine with API keys set diverges from CI in ways that have caused
multiple "works locally, fails in CI" incidents (and the reverse).

```bash
scripts/run_tests.sh                                  # full suite, CI-parity
scripts/run_tests.sh tests/gateway/                   # one directory
scripts/run_tests.sh tests/agent/test_foo.py::test_x  # one test
scripts/run_tests.sh -v --tb=long                     # pass-through pytest flags
```

### Why the wrapper (and why the old "just call pytest" doesn't work)

Five real sources of local-vs-CI drift the script closes:

| | Without wrapper | With wrapper |
|---|---|---|
| Provider API keys | Whatever is in your env (auto-detects pool) | All `*_API_KEY`/`*_TOKEN`/etc. unset |
| HOME / `~/.hermes/` | Your real config+auth.json | Temp dir per test |
| Timezone | Local TZ (PDT etc.) | UTC |
| Locale | Whatever is set | C.UTF-8 |
| xdist workers | `-n auto` = all cores (20+ on a workstation) | `-n 4` matching CI |

`tests/conftest.py` also enforces points 1-4 as an autouse fixture so ANY pytest
invocation (including IDE integrations) gets hermetic behavior — but the wrapper
is belt-and-suspenders.

### Running without the wrapper (only if you must)

If you can't use the wrapper (e.g. on Windows or inside an IDE that shells
pytest directly), at minimum activate the venv and pass `-n 4`:

```bash
source .venv/bin/activate   # or: source venv/bin/activate
python -m pytest tests/ -q -n 4
```

Worker count above 4 will surface test-ordering flakes that CI never sees.

Always run the full suite before pushing changes.

### Don't write change-detector tests

A test is a **change-detector** if it fails whenever data that is **expected
to change** gets updated — model catalogs, config version numbers,
enumeration counts, hardcoded lists of provider models. These tests add no
behavioral coverage; they just guarantee that routine source updates break
CI and cost engineering time to "fix."

**Do not write:**

```python
# catalog snapshot — breaks every model release
assert "gemini-2.5-pro" in _PROVIDER_MODELS["gemini"]
assert "MiniMax-M2.7" in models

# config version literal — breaks every schema bump
assert DEFAULT_CONFIG["_config_version"] == 21

# enumeration count — breaks every time a skill/provider is added
assert len(_PROVIDER_MODELS["huggingface"]) == 8
```

**Do write:**

```python
# behavior: does the catalog plumbing work at all?
assert "gemini" in _PROVIDER_MODELS
assert len(_PROVIDER_MODELS["gemini"]) >= 1

# behavior: does migration bump the user's version to current latest?
assert raw["_config_version"] == DEFAULT_CONFIG["_config_version"]

# invariant: no plan-only model leaks into the legacy list
assert not (set(moonshot_models) & coding_plan_only_models)

# invariant: every model in the catalog has a context-length entry
for m in _PROVIDER_MODELS["huggingface"]:
    assert m.lower() in DEFAULT_CONTEXT_LENGTHS_LOWER
```

The rule: if the test reads like a snapshot of current data, delete it. If
it reads like a contract about how two pieces of data must relate, keep it.
When a PR adds a new provider/model and you want a test, make the test
assert the relationship (e.g. "catalog entries all have context lengths"),
not the specific names.

Reviewers should reject new change-detector tests; authors should convert
them into invariants before re-requesting review.

function.name: memory
function.description:
Save durable information to persistent memory that survives across sessions. Memory is injected into future turns, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.

WHEN TO SAVE (do this proactively, don't wait to be asked):
- User corrects you or says 'remember this' / 'don't do that again'
- User shares a preference, habit, or personal detail (name, role, timezone, coding style)
- You discover something about the environment (OS, installed tools, project structure)
- You learn a convention, API quirk, or workflow specific to this user's setup
- You identify a stable fact that will be useful again in future sessions

PRIORITY: User preferences and corrections > environment facts > procedural knowledge. The most valuable memory prevents the user from having to repeat themselves.

Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts.
If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.

TWO TARGETS:
- 'user': who the user is -- name, role, preferences, communication style, pet peeves
- 'memory': your notes -- environment facts, project conventions, tool quirks, lessons learned

ACTIONS: add (new entry), replace (update existing -- old_text identifies it), remove (delete -- old_text identifies it).

SKIP: trivial/obvious info, things easily re-discovered, raw data dumps, and temporary task state.

target.description: Which memory store: 'memory' for personal notes, 'user' for user profile.

content.description: The entry content. Required for 'add' and 'replace'.

old_text.description: Short unique substring identifying the entry to replace or remove.

function.name: skill_manage
function.description:
Manage skills (create, update, delete). Skills are your procedural memory — reusable approaches for recurring task types. New skills go to ~/.hermes/skills/; existing skills can be modified wherever they live.

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string — preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite — major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

On delete, pass `absorbed_into=<umbrella>` when you're merging this skill's content into another one, or `absorbed_into=""` when you're pruning it with no forwarding target. This lets the curator tell consolidation from pruning without guessing, so downstream consumers (cron jobs that reference the old skill name, etc.) get updated correctly. The target you name in `absorbed_into` must already exist — create/patch the umbrella first, then delete.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

Pinned skills are protected from deletion only — skill_manage(action='delete') will refuse with a message pointing the user to `hermes curator unpin <name>`. Patches and edits go through on pinned skills so you can still improve them as pitfalls come up; pin only guards against irrecoverable loss.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

absorbed_into.description: For 'delete' only — declares intent so the curator can tell consolidation from pruning without guessing. Pass the umbrella skill name when this skill's content was merged into another (the target must already exist). Pass an empty string when the skill is truly stale and being pruned with no forwarding target. Omitting the arg on delete is supported for backward compatibility but downstream tooling (e.g. cron-job skill reference rewriting) will have to guess at intent.

function.name: skill_view
function.description: Skills allow for loading information about specific tasks and workflows, as well as scripts and templates. Load a skill's full content or access its linked files (references, templates, scripts). First call returns SKILL.md content plus a 'linked_files' dict showing available references/templates/scripts. To access those, call again with file_path parameter.

name.description: The skill name (use skills_list to see available skills). For plugin-provided skills, use the qualified form 'plugin:skill' (e.g. 'superpowers:writing-plans').

file_path.description: OPTIONAL: Path to a linked file within the skill (e.g., 'references/api.md', 'templates/config.yaml', 'scripts/validate.py'). Omit to get the main SKILL.md content.

function.name: skills_list
function.description: List available skills (name + description). Use skill_view(name) to load full content.

category.description: Optional category filter to narrow results

      - "3000"

    deploy:
      mode: replicated

          memory: 512M

{"output": " Sobre APA! | Anthro Party Argentina

Desde nuestro corazón, hacia el corazón de Argentina

Buscamos que pases un fin de semana excelente. Trabajamos arduamente para que te diviertas y formes nuevas amistades dentro del Fandom.

Anthro Party Argentina

Anthro Party Argentina o APA! es una convención furry organizada por y para miembros del furry fandom tanto de Argentina como del mundo. Nació de la pata del Staff del FCC (Furcamp Cordobés), como un proyecto a realizarse en un futuro próximo... y hoy ya está en marcha la realización de este sueño. Sean bienvenidos a esta fiesta, que es el Anthro Party Argentina.

Furcamp Cordobés

La idea del Furcamp Cordobés nació a finales de 2017. La primera edición fue el 25 al 27 de mayo de 2018, donde 19 furries asistieron. Hasta 2025, se realizaron anualmente buscando la diversión, compañerismo y estrechar lazos entre miembros del fandom provincial, nacional e internacional.

{"content": "     1|# Deep Analysis: Anthropartyargentina.com
     2|
     3|Full technical and UX audit of the current APA website.
     4|
     5|## 1. Technical Analysis
     6|
     7|### 1.1 Stack
     8|
     9|The site is built with **SvelteKit** (static site generation / prerender). The SvelteKit version appears to be 1.x/2.x based on the prerender URL leak. It uses Svelte's component model with a standard SvelteKit layout structure.
    10|
    11|**Build output**: Static HTML files with Svelte hydration JS bundles.
    12|
    13|### 1.2 Critical Issues
    14|
    15|#### Issue 1: Canonical URL Leak (SEO Critical)
    16|
    17|Pages other than the home route emit:
    18|
    19|```html
    20|<link rel="canonical" href="http://sveltekit-prerender/booking" />
    21|```
    22|
    23|`http://sveltekit-prerender` is SvelteKit's internal build hostname. This leaks to production because the `prerender.origin` config in `svelte.config.js` is not set. **Impact**: search engines see the wrong canonical URL. **Fix**: add `prerender: { origin: 'https://anthropartyargentina.com' }` to the svelte config.
    24|
    25|#### Issue 2: Missing Per-Page Title Tags
    26|
    27|All pages return `<title>APA - Anthro Party Argentina</title>` — the default layout title. The `svelte:head` component for setting per-page titles is either missing or not firing. This means:
    28|- Each page has identical title tags
    29|- Search results show the same title for every page
    30|- Browser tabs are indistinguishable
    31|
    32|**Fix**: Add `<svelte:head><title>Page Title | APA</title></svelte:head>` to each route.
    33|
    34|#### Issue 3: Massive Image Payload (Performance Critical)
    35|
    36|| Image | Size | Format | Notes |
    37||---|---|---|---|
    38|| `honors_artwork.png` | 8.3 MB | PNG | Should be WebP @80% → ~500KB |
    39|| `landing_illustration_banner.png` | 7.4 MB | PNG | Should be WebP → ~450KB |
    40|| `about_hero_photo.jpg` | 4.8 MB | JPEG | Can compress to ~300KB |
    41|| `furcamp_shirt_design.png` | 2.5 MB | PNG | Should be WebP → ~150KB |
    42|| `hero_characters.png` | 0.9 MB | PNG | Should be WebP → ~100KB |
    43|| **Total** | **~24 MB** | | Should be **~1.5 MB** |
    44|
    45|**Mobile performance**: On 3G, this is a 20+ second load time. Google Core Web Vitals will penalize this heavily. **Fix**: convert all raster images to WebP at 80% quality. SVG files (logos) are fine as-is.
    46|
    47|#### Issue 4: Duplicate Hotel Room Image
    48|
    49|The "Deluxe Twin" and "Classic TWIN" room cards on `/booking` use the **identical image URL**:
    50|
    51|```
    52|https://images-new.pxsol.com/[REDACTED_TOKEN]/...
    53|```
    54|
    55|This means guests comparing room tiers see the same photo for two different room types. It's almost certainly a placeholder error.
    56|
    57|#### Issue 5: Sitemap Returns HTML
    58|
    59|`/sitemap.xml` returns `text/html` with the homepage content instead of actual XML sitemap. This means search engines get no sitemap guidance.
    60|
    61|#### Issue 6: No robots.txt
    62|
    63|No `/robots.txt` found. While not critical, it means no crawl budget directives.
    64|
    65|### 1.3 Technical Scores (Estimated)
    66|
    67|| Metric | Current | Target |
    68||---|---|---|
    69|| Lighthouse Performance (mobile) | ~35-45 | 90+ |
    70|| First Contentful Paint | ~4-6s | <1.5s |
    71|| Total Page Weight | ~25 MB | <2 MB |
    72|| SEO Score | ~60 | 100 |
    73|| Accessibility | ~50-60 | 90+ |
    74|
    75|---
    76|
    77|## 2. UX / Content Audit
    78|
    79|### 2.1 Navigation
    80|
    81|**Current**: 3 items — HOME, HOTEL, SOBRE NOSOTROS
    82|
    83|**Industry standard** for furry conventions (ArFF, Confuror, etc.):
    84|
    85|| Page | APA has it? | Needed? |
    86||---|---|---|
    87|| Home | ✓ | ✓ |
    88|| Tickets / Pricing | ✗ | **Critical** |
    89|| Hotel / Booking | ✓ | ✓ |
    90|| Schedule / Cronograma | ✗ | **Critical** |
    91|| Dealers Den | ✗ (only in rules) | **High** |
    92|| About | ✓ | ✓ |
    93|| FAQ | ✗ | **High** |
    94|| Rules / Reglamento | ✓ (in footer) | ✓ |
    95|| Gallery | ✗ | Medium |
    96|| Contact | ✗ | Medium |
    97|| Privacy | ✗ | Low |
    98|
    99|### 2.2 User Flow Pain Points
   100|
   101|1. **No pricing visibility**: Users must click "Inscribite" → get sent to `/login` before they can see ticket prices. This is a conversion killer — curious visitors won't register just to see a price.
   102|
   103|2. **No schedule**: People planning travel (especially international) need to know when things start/end to book flights and hotels.
   104|
   105|3. **No FAQ**: The reglamento has the info but it's dense legal text. A FAQ with simple Q&A would dramatically reduce support burden.
   106|
   107|4. **Single CTA type**: Every call-to-action on the site is "Inscribite" → `/login`. Different sections need different actions (Book Hotel, See Schedule, Apply as Artist, etc.)
   108|
   109|### 2.3 Accessibility Issues
   110|
   111|- All social media icons in footer use `alt="redes sociales"` (identical non-descriptive alt text)
   112|- Team member avatars use `alt="Image 1"` through `alt="Image 5"`
   113|- No skip-to-content link
   114|- Color contrast: purple on dark background is close to minimum WCAG AA thresholds
   115|
   116|---
   117|
   118|## 3. Improvement Plan
   119|
   120|### Phase 1: Quick Fixes (2-3 hours)
   121|
   122|| Fix | Effort | Impact |
   123||---|---|---|
   124|| Convert PNGs → WebP | 1 hr | **Massive** — cuts load time 80% |
   125|| Fix canonical URL in svelte.config | 5 min | SEO critical |
   126|| Add per-page title tags | 30 min | SEO + UX |
   127|| Fix hotel image duplicate | 10 min | Trust |
   128|| Add alt text to icons | 15 min | Accessibility |
   129|
   130|### Phase 2: Content Pages (4-6 hours)
   131|
   132|| Page | Effort | Notes |
   133||---|---|---|
   134|| FAQ page | 1 hr | Extract Q&A from existing rules |
   135|| Schedule/Cronograma | 1 hr | Even placeholder is fine |
   136|| Tickets/Pricing | 1 hr | Compare tiers, payment info |
   137|| Dealers Den page | 1 hr | Application info + rules |
   138|| Contact page | 30 min | Social links, hotel booking info |
   139|
   140|### Phase 3: Polish (2-4 hours)
   141|
   142|| Improvement | Effort |
   143||---|---|
   144|| Full navigation with all pages | 1 hr |
   145|| Gallery page with FCC photos | 1 hr |
   146|| Privacy policy | 30 min |
   147|| Bluesky link | 5 min |
   148|| sitemap.xml | 30 min |

{"output": "Reglamento | APA!

Reglamento

Versión 2.2 — Actualizado 25/12/2025

Reglamento General

1. Admisión y Credenciales

Art. 1. Evento estrictamente para mayores de 18 años. Se exigirá DNI físico en puerta.

Art. 2. El Staff tiene autoridad final. Sus indicaciones son obligatorias.

Art. 3. Entradas adquiribles solo en anthropartyargentina.com. Son transferibles.

Art. 4. Se permite regalar entradas hasta 90 días antes del evento.

Art. 5. Obligatorio portar Badge (colgante) y Pulsera de Seguridad en todo momento.

Art. 6. El extravío del Badge o Pulsera tiene un costo de reposición.

2. Pagos, Reembolso y Asistencia

Art. 7. Medios de pago: MercadoPago (ARS) o PayPal (USD).

Art. 8. Derecho de arrepentimiento de 5 días corridos (Ley 24.240). Pasado ese plazo, no hay devolución.

Art. 10. La compra implica aceptación total del reglamento. Derecho de admisión reservado.

3. Prohibiciones

Art. 11-16. Prohibido: bloquear salidas de emergencia, manipular equipos, clavar/pegar en paredes, usar fuego/chispas, fumar/vapear en espacios cerrados, sustancias psicoactivas, comida externa (excepto agua), correr, actividad sexual en áreas comunes, confetti/glitter, bloqueadores de señal, drones, discurso de odio, propaganda política.

4. Consentimiento, Higiene y Vestimenta

Art. 17. APA! es un espacio de tolerancia, cordialidad y respeto mutuo.

Art. 19. El "NO" es definitivo. Prohibido tocar fursuits sin permiso explícito. No jalar colas, orejas o pelaje.

Art. 20. Se exige ducha diaria y uso de desodorante.

Art. 23. Prohibida la exhibición de ropa interior, pañales o indumentaria sugestiva en zonas generales. Prohibición total de armas reales y réplicas realistas.

5. Sanciones

Art. 24. Advertencia verbal (3 = expulsión), advertencia escrita, o expulsión directa.

Art. 25. Tolerancia cero a la agresión física o verbal. Expulsión inmediata y definitiva + acciones legales.

6. Disposiciones Finales

Art. 26. APA! no se responsabiliza por pérdida, hurto o daño de objetos personales.

Art. 29. La asistencia implica cesión de derechos de imagen para fines promocionales.

El reglamento completo incluye también anexos para Dealers Den, Segmentos AD y Competencia de Baile.

{"output": "Privacidad | APA!

Política de Privacidad

En APA! (Anthro Party Argentina) nos tomamos tu privacidad en serio. Esta política describe cómo recopilamos, usamos y protegemos tu información personal.

Información que recopilamos

Al registrarte y comprar entradas, recopilamos: nombre, dirección de email, edad/verificación de mayoría de edad, y datos de pago (procesados de forma segura por MercadoPago o PayPal).

Cómo usamos tu información

Usamos tu información para procesar tu entrada, comunicarte información del evento, verificar tu edad en puerta, y mejorar la experiencia del evento.

Fotografías y video

Al asistir al evento, aceptas que puedas ser fotografiado o filmado con fines promocionales. Si no deseas aparecer, comunicate con el staff durante el evento.

Seguridad

No compartimos tu información personal con terceros excepto cuando sea necesario para procesar pagos o cumplir con la ley.

Contacto

Si tenés preguntas sobre tus datos, contactanos a través de Telegram (@Pumy_apa) o en el evento.

Done. Here's what changed:

**Homepage** — added 4 new sections:
- Graduados de Honor now shows the full honors_artwork.png (8.4MB artwork from Cabura & Koshka) with links to @Cabura_art and @koshkafursuits on X
- Landing illustration banner (landing_illustration_banner.png, 7.5MB) as a side-by-side section with "Una furcon con historia" and link to gallery
- Furcamp Cordobés section with furcamp_logo.svg, furcamp_shirt_design.png, and about_hero_photo.jpg — links back to the team page
- CTA final now has both "Inscribite ahora" and "Galería del evento" buttons

**About page** — added Pumy mascota section (about_pumy_character.png) with @Pumy_apa contact, plus a staff & community gallery grid with the hero photo and furcamp shirt

**New gallery page** at `/gallery` — 15 assets displayed in a responsive grid: honors artwork, landing illustration, hero characters, team photos, hotel shots, Pumy, furcamp design, and all 5 team avatars. Includes community callout with Instagram/X/Bluesky links, artist credit links to Cabura and Koshka, and social network buttons

**Footer** — added Bluesky icon (SVG inline), Instagram moved to first position, all labels fixed, gallery link added to the "Sobre APA!" section

**Unused assets now used**: honors_artwork.png, landing_illustration_banner.png, about_pumy_character.png, furcamp_logo.svg, furcamp_shirt_design.png, all 5 team avatars, booking_hotel_exterior.jpg

function.name: patch

{"new_string": "

En APA! (Anthro Party Argentina) nos tomamos tu privacidad en serio. Esta política describe cómo recopilamos, usamos y protegemos tu información personal.

Información que recopilamos

Al registrarte y comprar entradas, recopilamos: nombre, dirección de email, edad/verificación de mayoría de edad, y datos de pago (procesados de forma segura por MercadoPago o PayPal).

Cómo usamos tu información

Usamos tu información para procesar tu entrada, comunicarte información del evento, verificar tu edad en puerta, y mejorar la experiencia del evento.

Fotografías y video

Al asistir al evento, aceptas que puedas ser fotografiado o filmado con fines promocionales. Si no deseas aparecer, comunicate con el staff durante el evento.

Seguridad

No compartimos tu información personal con terceros excepto cuando sea necesario para procesar pagos o cumplir con la ley.

Contacto

Si tenés preguntas sobre tus datos, contactanos a través de Telegram (@Pumy_apa) o en el evento.

Reglamento completo → Preguntas frecuentes → Contacto →

The goal was to gather information to build a website for the business. The conversation involved deploying this website (`elviajero-comercio`) as a standalone subdomain (`el-viajero.paragu-ai.com`) on the existing ParaguAI infrastructure.

**2. What actions were taken and what the outcomes were**

- **DNS Configuration:** The user added an A record for `el-viajero` pointing to `72.61.44.159` in the Cloudflare dashboard for `paragu-ai.com`. This was necessary because the Cloudflare API token used by the assistant lacked DNS write permissions.
- **Traefik Router Update:** The assistant updated the Traefik routing rule in the `docker-compose.yml` from `Host(\`viajero.paragu-ai.com\`)` to `Host(\`el-viajero.paragu-ai.com\`)` to match the DNS record.

- **SSL Certificate Issue:** Traefik initially returned a 526 error (invalid SSL) because Let's Encrypt was rate-limited due to multiple failed attempts for the old `viajero.paragu-ai.com` domain (which didn't exist at the time). After the DNS propagated and the correct host rule was applied, the certificate was obtained.
- **Final Verification:** Both sites were confirmed to be returning **200 OK**:
    - `https://dayah.paragu-ai.com` ✅
    - `https://el-viajero.paragu-ai.com` ✅

**3. Key decisions, solutions found, or conclusions reached**

- **Tenant Separation Model:** The established pattern for deploying client sites is:
    1.  Own repository (e.g., `Ai-Whisperers/elviajero-comercio`).
    2.  Own Docker service in the Swarm.
    3.  Subdomain on `paragu-ai.com` (e.g., `{client}.paragu-ai.com`).
    4.  Manual Cloudflare A record (due to API token limitations).
- **Deployment Type:** `elviajero-comercio` is a **standalone Next.js app** (not an nginx proxy like `dayah-litworks`). It builds and renders independently.
- **Infrastructure Limitation:** The Cloudflare API token (`CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN`) only has `zone:read` scope, not `DNS:write`. All DNS records must be added manually via the Cloudflare dashboard.

**4. Any specific commands, files, URLs, or technical details that were important**

- **Repository:** `github.com/Ai-Whisperers/elviajero-comercio`
- **Live URL:** `https://el-viajero.paragu-ai.com`

- **Key File:** `/tmp/elviajero-comercio/docker-compose.yml` (Traefik labels)
- **Key Command (to update service labels):** `docker service update --label-add traefik.http.routers.viajero.rule=Host(\`el-viajero.paragu-ai.com\`) elviajero-comercio_web`
- **Key Command (to remove old service):** `docker service rm elviajero_web`
- **Error:** `HTTP/2 526` (invalid SSL) due to Let's Encrypt rate limiting.
- **Error:** `Router defined multiple times with different configurations` (due to duplicate services).

- **Unresolved:** The assistant attempted to push a fix to the `README.md` and `CLAUDE.md` files to update the subdomain URL, but the push was rejected due to a non-fast-forward error and merge conflicts. The remote repository already had the latest content, so the local fix was abandoned. The documentation on the remote repo still references the old `viajero.paragu-ai.com` URL.
- **Notable:** The user is actively filling out a comprehensive business questionnaire, indicating a move towards a more formalized and feature-rich web presence (including e-commerce with a cart, online payment, and user login/registration)."}, {"session_id": "20260506_060944_d47885", "when": "May 06, 2026 at 06:09 AM", "source": "cli", "model": "deepseek-chat", "summary": "Here is a summary of the conversation regarding the Ai-Whisperers/ParaguAI organization architecture on GitHub.

### 1. User's Goal
The user wanted to complete a list of high-priority tasks to harden and streamline the Ai-Whisperers GitHub organization. The core objectives were to:
- Fix broken automation (cron jobs).
- Remove human bottlenecks from the development workflow (branch protection).
- Publish internal packages for use across client repos.
- Refresh the client template.
- Begin addressing content gaps for a key client (Golden Visa Advisory).

### 2. Actions Taken & Outcomes
The assistant executed a series of tasks, updating a todo list as it progressed.

- **Cron Jobs:** Investigated 22 failing cron jobs (Solstein, IC, lead-scout). Found the root cause was a missing `api_key` in the Hermes config (`config.yaml`), even though the key existed in the `.env` file. The assistant wrote the key to the config file. The user then instructed the assistant to delete all 22 jobs, keeping only 5 business-relevant ones. This was completed.
- **Branch Protection:** The user wanted AI to be able to push directly without human approval. The assistant removed the human review requirement from 6 key repos (elviajero, fun4me, paragu-ai-builder, solstein-v2, base, ma-research-pipeline), enabling `allow_force_pushes` and `allow_deletions`. Workflow permissions were set to `read` for security.
- **Local Repo Sync:** The assistant identified 17 client repos with un-pushed local changes. It committed and force-pushed these changes to GitHub. One repo (fun4me) was blocked by the newly relaxed branch protection, which was working as intended.
- **npm Publish Pipeline:** The assistant added a `changesets` configuration and a `publish.yml` GitHub Action to the `@ai-whisperers/base` monorepo. This pipeline is designed to create release PRs or publish packages to GitHub Packages on pushes to `main`.
- **Template Refresh:** The `template-nextjs-client` was updated. Its `@ai-whisperers/*` dependencies were changed from local `file:` paths to `^0.1.0` semver versions. An `.npmrc` file was added for GitHub Packages authentication.
- **Golden Visa Content:** The assistant analyzed the Golden Visa Advisory site and found that 5 of its 7 languages (pt, fr, it, de, nl) had no content. It generated and committed full translations for all sections (entry, investor, business, FAQ) for these 5 languages.
- **Remaining Work:** The assistant created a plan file (`/root/remaining-work.md`) for the two largest remaining tasks: Superspuma features and elviajero component extraction.

### 3. Key Decisions & Conclusions
- **AI-First Workflow:** The user explicitly decided to remove human approval gates, giving the AI full control over pushing to `main` on key repos. The assistant implemented this.
- **Package Registry:** The decision was made to use GitHub Packages (not npmjs) for publishing the `@ai-whisperers/*` packages, as the organization was already authenticated there.
- **Automation Cleanup:** The user decided to delete the majority of the cron jobs (Solstein pipeline, IC, lead-scout) rather than fix them, simplifying the system.

### 4. Specific Technical Details
- **Cron Fix:** The fix involved writing the `DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` environment variable value into the `api_key` field of the `model` section in `/root/.hermes/config.yaml`.
- **Branch Protection API:** The assistant used the GitHub API to update branch protection. It initially failed with a `422` error due to sending boolean/integer values as strings (e.g., `"true"` instead of `true`). The fix was to use proper JSON types in the API call.
- **GitHub Environments:** An attempt to create a `production` environment with a `wait_timer` failed because the feature requires a paid GitHub plan. The environment was created without the timer.
- **Template Dependencies:** The template's `package.json` was updated to use `"@ai-whisperers/ui": "^0.1.0"` instead of `"file:/root/ai-whisperers-base/packages/ui"`.
- **Content File:** The Golden Visa content was stored in a single JSON file (`src/content/data.json`). The assistant added 429 lines of new translation data to this file.

### 5. Unresolved Items & Notable Points
- **Unresolved:** Two major tasks remain and were saved to a plan file (`/root/remaining-work.md`):
    1.  **Superspuma Features:** Building a product quiz, bundle builder, and B2B portal.
    2.  **El Viajero Extraction:** Extracting 104 components from the elviajero-comercio repo into reusable `@aiw/*` packages.
- **Notable:** The assistant attempted to use a `delegate_task` tool to parallelize work but failed because the delegation API key was not configured. It then used `execute_code` for batch processing instead.
- **Notable:** The assistant discovered that the `template-nextjs-client` on GitHub was significantly smaller (99 files) than the local version (435 files), but the difference was only `.next/` build artifacts, not actual source code. The template was already complete and synced."}, {"session_id": "20260501_232410_dac2646a", "when": "May 01, 2026 at 11:24 PM", "source": "whatsapp", "model": "deepseek-chat", "summary": "Here is a summary of the conversation, focusing on the organizational architecture of the `Ai-Whisperers` GitHub organization and the `ParaguAI` project.

### Summary of Conversation: Ai-Whisperers Org Architecture & ParaguAI

**1. What the user asked about or wanted to accomplish**

The user was working on deploying a website for "Stroopwafel Huis," a Dutch coffee shop concept in Paraguay. The core task was to push a newly built Next.js website to the existing `Ai-Whisperers/stroopwafel-huis` repository on GitHub and deploy it. The underlying goal was to utilize the `paragu-ai-builder` pattern (a multi-tenant website generation engine) to create a production-ready site.

**2. What actions were taken and what the outcomes were**

- **Git Push Attempts:** The assistant first attempted to push the new code using an SSH key, which failed because the key was read-only. It then switched to an HTTPS token, which also failed because the repository was archived.
- **Repository Unarchived:** The assistant successfully unarchived the `Ai-Whisperers/stroopwafel-huis` repository via the GitHub API.
- **Branch Conflict Resolution:** The remote repository's default branch was `master`, while the local branch was `main`. The assistant renamed the local branch to `master` and attempted a push, which was rejected because the remote had existing content. The assistant then performed a **force push** (`git push --force`), which was approved by the user, overwriting the remote history with the new website code.
- **Docker Build & Deploy:** The assistant built a Docker image (`stroopwafel-huis:prod`) and deployed it as a Docker Swarm service (`stroopwafel-huis_web`) with 2 replicas.
- **Verification & Debugging:** Initial `curl` requests returned a 404 error. The assistant debugged this by checking the container's internal file system, confirming the content (`es.json`) was present. It then ran `curl` directly inside the container, which returned the full, correct HTML for the `/menu` page. The 404 was attributed to the load balancer hitting the service before the second replica was fully ready.

**3. Key decisions, solutions found, or conclusions reached**

- **Force Push was Necessary:** The decision to force push was made to replace the old repository content (business planning docs) with the new website code, as the remote history was irrelevant.
- **Deployment Architecture:** The site was deployed using a Docker Swarm stack with Traefik as a reverse proxy, running 2 replicas of the Next.js application.
- **Site is Live:** The website was confirmed to be working correctly, serving a full, multi-page site with a Dutch Orange theme, a complete menu (48+ items), and SEO metadata.
- **Next Steps Identified:** The assistant provided a clear list of remaining tasks for the user, including DNS configuration (pointing `stroopwafelhuis.paragu-ai.com` to the server IP), updating placeholder WhatsApp numbers, adding brand images, and setting up a real domain.

**4. Specific commands, files, URLs, or technical details that were important**

- **Repository:** `Ai-Whisperers/stroopwafel-huis` (Private, unarchived)
- **Key Error:** `"ERROR: The key you are authenticating with has been marked as read only."` (SSH) and `"remote: This repository was archived so it is read-only."` (HTTPS)
- **Critical Command:** `git push --force origin master` (Approved by user)
- **Deployment:** Docker Swarm service `stroopwafel-huis_web` using image `stroopwafel-huis:prod`
- **Technology Stack:** Next.js 16, Tailwind v4, Docker, Docker Swarm, Traefik
- **Content Source:** `src/content/es.json` (Spanish language content)
- **Target URL:** `https://stroopwafelhuis.paragu-ai.com`
- **Server IP:** `72.61.44.159`
- **AGENTS.md File:** The assistant discovered and read the `AGENTS.md` file, which is the core architectural document for the **ParaguAI Builder**. This file defines the project's structure, rules (e.g., "DO NOT upgrade Tailwind to v4"), and workflows for AI agents.

**5. Anything left unresolved or notable**

- **Unresolved:** The user asked "Any more things to work on?" at the end of the transcript. The assistant began checking the site's current state (finding it healthy) but the conversation was truncated before a final answer was given. The assistant's previous list of "next steps" (DNS, WhatsApp number, images, real domain) remained as pending tasks for the user.
- **Notable:** The discovery of the `AGENTS.md` file is highly significant. It reveals that the `Ai-Whisperers` organization has a sophisticated, AI-first development methodology. The `ParaguAI Builder` is not just a single website but a **multi-tenant, multi-locale website generation engine** for Paraguayan businesses. It uses a token-based theming system, 21 reusable section components, and a specific file structure for adding new business types (e.g., `dentist`, `mechanic`). This conversation demonstrates the practical application of this architecture to create a specific business site."}], "count": 3, "sessions_searched": 3}

{"output": "# ParaguAI — Operations Base

**Single source of truth.** Repos, clients, sites, infra, cron, contacts, issues.

## Index

| File | What's Inside |
|------|---------------|
| [client-repos.md](client-repos.md) | Every repo: client, framework, last commit, GH link, status, deep details |
| [clients.md](clients.md) | All clients with URLs, contacts, tiers |
| [cron.md](cron.md) | All 22 cron jobs with schedules and status |
| [infrastructure.md](infrastructure.md) | Docker Swarm, Traefik, network map |
| [issues.md](issues.md) | Priority-ordered 🔴🟡🟢 issues |
| [repos.md](repos.md) | Full repo index by category |

## Quick Stats

- **19 live websites** on *.paragu-ai.com + 2 on *.sunstein.cloud
- **22 Docker Swarm services** running on 1 VPS
- **30+ Git repos** (16 client sites, 9 platform, 5 Hermes, rest research/auto)

function.name: write_file
function.arguments:
{"content": "# Ai-Whisperers / ParaguAI — Organization Analysis & MCP Strategy

## Organization Profile

### Business
**ParaguAI** — AI-powered website builder for Paraguayan businesses. Full-service digital agency:
- 19+ live client websites on *.paragu-ai.com
- Multi-tenant Next.js platform (paragu-ai-builder) for beauty/wellness
- Custom standalone sites for law firms, clinics, retail, artists
- WhatsApp Business AI agent integration
- M&A research (Solstein) and lead generation

### Tech Stack
- **Frontend:** Next.js 15/16, SvelteKit, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS 4
- **Backend:** Python, Java (Quarkus), Node.js
- **Infrastructure:** Docker Swarm, Traefik, Nginx Alpine (~54MB images)
- **Database:** Supabase (PostgreSQL), SQLite (Hermes)
- **AI:** Hermes Agent, Claude Code, DeepSeek, LiteLLM → Groq
- **CI/CD:** GitHub Actions, Docker builds
- **DNS/CDN:** Cloudflare (proxied)
- **Package Registry:** GitHub Packages (@ai-whisperers/*)
- **Hosting:** 1 VPS (72.61.44.159, Hostinger)

### Repository Count
- **Ai-Whisperers org:** 100 non-fork repos (37 active, rest archived/research)
- **IvanWeissVanDerPol personal:** 30 repos (active tools, experiments)
- **Total:** ~130 repos managed

### Services Running (Docker Swarm)
- 19 client websites (nginx/Next.js)
- Traefik (reverse proxy, SSL)
- Grafana + Prometheus + Node Exporter (monitoring)
- Evolution API + Redis (WhatsApp Business)
- PostgreSQL, OpenWebUI, Portainer
- Space Agent, Hermes Agent (gateway)

## MCP Server Analysis

### Current MCPs Installed
1. **@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem** — File system access
2. **@modelcontextprotocol/server-sequential-thinking** — Multi-step reasoning
3. **Supabase MCP (oclient OAuth)** — For Supabase queries

### High-Value MCPs to Install

#### PRIORITY 1: GitHub MCP Server
**Package:** `@modelcontextprotocol/server-github`
**Value:** Direct API access to all GitHub operations — issues, PRs, repos, code search, review
**Why:** We manage 130 repos. Currently use gh CLI which has auth quirks. MCP gives structured tool access.
**Install:** `npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-github <GH_TOKEN>`

#### PRIORITY 2: Context7 MCP (Docs)
**Package:** `@upstash/context7-mcp`
**Value:** On-demand docs for 500+ libraries — Next.js, Svelte, Tailwind, Docker, etc.
**Why:** We build with Next.js/SvelteKit daily. Eliminates browser searches for API docs.
**Install:** `npx -y @upstash/context7-mcp`

#### PRIORITY 3: Cloudflare MCP
**Package:** Needs custom MCP server or @anthropic/mcp-server-cloudflare
**Value:** Automate DNS record management — currently manual, biggest operational pain point
**Why:** Every new client site needs DNS A record. Currently manual Cloudflare dashboard.
**Install:** Requires CF API token with DNS:write scope.

#### PRIORITY 4: PostgreSQL MCP
**Package:** `@modelcontextprotocol/server-postgres`
**Value:** Direct database queries without pg CLI
**Why:** We have Supabase + local PostgreSQL. Useful for data analysis.
**Install:** `npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-postgres [REDACTED_DB_URL] How to Configure in Hermes
Add to `~/.hermes/config.yaml`:
```yaml
mcp_servers:
  github:
    command: npx
    args:
      - -y
      - '@modelcontextprotocol/server-github'
      - <GH_TOKEN>
  context7:
    command: npx
    args:
      - -y
      - '@upstash/context7-mcp'
  # cloudflare:
  #   command: npx
  #   args:
  #     - -y
  #     - '@anthropic/mcp-server-cloudflare'
  # postgres:
  #   command: npx
  #   args:
  #     - -y
  #     - '@modelcontextprotocol/server-postgres'
  #     - '[REDACTED_DB_URL]'
```

## Recommendation

**Install now:** GitHub MCP + Context7 MCP
**Install when token available:** Cloudflare MCP (needs DNS:write API token)
**Install optionally:** PostgreSQL MCP (when Supabase queries needed frequently)

function.name: execute_code
function.arguments:
{"code": "# Research top MCPs for our stack
import subprocess, json

# Extended search for MCPs that match our org needs
checks = {
    "Notion": "@notionhq/notion-mcp-server",
    "Brave Search": "@brave/brave-search-mcp-server",
    "Docker": "@hypnosis/docker-mcp-server",
    "Exa Search": "exa-mcp-server",
    "Figma": "figma-mcp",
    "You.com": "@youdotcom-oss/mcp",
    "Vercel": None,
    "Stripe": "stripe-mcp",
    "Sentry": "sentry-mcp",
    "Google Maps": "google-maps-mcp",
    "Puppeteer": "@anthropic/mcp-server-puppeteer",
    "Playwright": "@executeautomation/playwright-mcp-server",
}

for name, pkg in sorted(checks.items()):
    if not pkg:
        continue
    r = subprocess.run(["npm", "view", pkg, "version", "description"], 
                       capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=10)
    if r.returncode == 0:
        ver = r.stdout.split('\n')[0] if r.stdout.strip() else '?'
        desc = r.stdout.split('\n')[1][:80] if len(r.stdout.split('\n')) > 1 else '?'
        print(f"{name:15s} v{ver:12s} {desc}")
    else:
        print(f"{name:15s} NOT FOUND")

# Filter for what's actually useful for us
print("\n\n=== TOP 5 FOR OUR ORG (sorted by value) ===")
print("1. Cloudflare MCP — DNS, Workers, KV management (installing now)")
print("2. Notion MCP — read/write Notion docs (we use Notion for client docs)")
print("3. Brave Search MCP — better web search than DuckDuckGo")
print("4. Exa Search MCP — structured web search (research)")
print("5. Stripe MCP — payment data (client payments)")
"}

from hermes_tools import terminal

# 3. Check for hermes update

print(f"version: {r3['output'][:200]}")
"}

Review the conversation above and update the skill library. Be ACTIVE — most sessions produce at least one skill update, even if small. A pass that does nothing is a missed learning opportunity, not a neutral outcome.

Target shape of the library: CLASS-LEVEL skills, each with a rich SKILL.md and a `references/` directory for session-specific detail. Not a long flat list of narrow one-session-one-skill entries. This shapes HOW you update, not WHETHER you update.

Signals to look for (any one of these warrants action):
  • User corrected your style, tone, format, legibility, or verbosity. Frustration signals like 'stop doing X', 'this is too verbose', 'don't format like this', 'why are you explaining', 'just give me the answer', 'you always do Y and I hate it', or an explicit 'remember this' are FIRST-CLASS skill signals, not just memory signals. Update the relevant skill(s) to embed the preference so the next session starts already knowing.
  • User corrected your workflow, approach, or sequence of steps. Encode the correction as a pitfall or explicit step in the skill that governs that class of task.
  • Non-trivial technique, fix, workaround, debugging path, or tool-usage pattern emerged that a future session would benefit from. Capture it.
  • A skill that got loaded or consulted this session turned out to be wrong, missing a step, or outdated. Patch it NOW.

Preference order — prefer the earliest action that fits, but do pick one when a signal above fired:
  1. UPDATE A CURRENTLY-LOADED SKILL. Look back through the conversation for skills the user loaded via /skill-name or you read via skill_view. If any of them covers the territory of the new learning, PATCH that one first. It is the skill that was in play, so it's the right one to extend.
  2. UPDATE AN EXISTING UMBRELLA (via skills_list + skill_view). If no loaded skill fits but an existing class-level skill does, patch it. Add a subsection, a pitfall, or broaden a trigger.
  3. ADD A SUPPORT FILE under an existing umbrella. Skills can be packaged with three kinds of support files — use the right directory per kind:
     • `references/<topic>.md` — session-specific detail (error transcripts, reproduction recipes, provider quirks) AND condensed knowledge banks: quoted research, API docs, external authoritative excerpts, or domain notes you found while working on the problem. Write it concise and for the value of the task, not as a full mirror of upstream docs.
     • `templates/<name>.<ext>` — starter files meant to be copied and modified (boilerplate configs, scaffolding, a known-good example the agent can `reproduce with modifications`).
     • `scripts/<name>.<ext>` — statically re-runnable actions the skill can invoke directly (verification scripts, fixture generators, deterministic probes, anything the agent should run rather than hand-type each time).
     Add support files via skill_manage action=write_file with file_path starting 'references/', 'templates/', or 'scripts/'. The umbrella's SKILL.md should gain a one-line pointer to any new support file so future agents know it exists.
  4. CREATE A NEW CLASS-LEVEL UMBRELLA SKILL when no existing skill covers the class. The name MUST be at the class level. The name MUST NOT be a specific PR number, error string, feature codename, library-alone name, or 'fix-X / debug-Y / audit-Z-today' session artifact. If the proposed name only makes sense for today's task, it's wrong — fall back to (1), (2), or (3).

User-preference embedding (important): when the user expressed a style/format/workflow preference, the update belongs in the SKILL.md body, not just in memory. Memory captures 'who the user is and what the current situation and state of your operations are'; skills capture 'how to do this class of task for this user'. When they complain about how you handled a task, the skill that governs that task needs to carry the lesson.

If you notice two existing skills that overlap, note it in your reply — the background curator handles consolidation at scale.

'Nothing to save.' is a real option but should NOT be the default. If the session ran smoothly with no corrections and produced no new technique, just say 'Nothing to save.' and stop. Otherwise, act.

2. **Anthro Party Argentina deployment** — fixed the gallery page 404 issue (missing prerender entry + missing +page.ts), enhanced navigation structure with full navbar and cross-page links. This is a common pattern with SvelteKit static sites.
3. **Organization analysis** — we generated a full org profile with repo counts, services, tech stack. Reusable pattern.

standalone output, Docker builds, and runtime routing." version: 1.0.0 author: Hermes Agent license: MIT metadata: hermes: tags: [nextjs, deployment, docker, debugging, build, routes, pages-router, app-router] related_skills: [systematic-debugging, ecommerce-codebase-audit, kanban-orchestrator] --- # Next.js Deployment & Debugging ## Overview This skill covers debugging and fixing Next.js deployment issues: build failures, routes not registering, standalone output problems, Docker build issues with GitHub Packages, and hybrid App Router / Pages Router troubleshooting. ### `pages/` and `app/` Directory Conflict When switching from App Router to Pages Router, a **stale root-level `app/` directory** (not `src/app/`) will cause: ``` pages and app directories should be under the same folder ``` **Fix:** Remove the stale `app/` directory at the repo root: ```bash rm -rf app ``` Always check for both `src/app/` AND `app/` when cleaning up. ## Working Patterns for Section-Based Content Sites When building a site that serves JSON-driven sectioned content from `getServerSideProps`, use this architecture: ### File structure ``` src/pages/index.tsx ← home page (SSR via getServerSideProps) src/pages/[slug].tsx ← all other pages (SSR via getServerSideProps) src/pages/admin/content.tsx ← admin editor (static, uses client-kit) src/pages/_document.tsx ← custom HTML shell src/pages/globals.css ← tailwind-based theme (not _app.tsx styling) src/components/sections.tsx ← all visual section components src/components/Header.tsx ← navigation bar src/components/Footer.tsx ← footer src/components/content.ts ← content resolver helper ``` ### Component section mapping pattern Each section in the page config JSON (e.g. `{id:"hero",variant:"split",content:"home.hero"}`) maps to a component via a `SECTION_MAP` record: ```tsx const SECTION_MAP: Record = { 'hero': HeroSection, 'stats-counter': StatsSection, 'trust-signals': TrustSection, 'programs-comparison': ProgramsSection, 'services': ServicesSection, 'tax-savings-calculator': TaxCalculatorSection, 'why-destination': WhyCountrySection, 'features': FeaturesSection, 'process-timeline': ProcessSection, 'testimonials': TestimonialsSection, 'cta-banner': CtaBanner, } ``` ### Page content builder Build a flat pageContent object so components don't need to understand dot-path resolution: ```tsx function buildPageContent(base: any): any { const pc: any = {} for (const sec of sections) { const key = sec.content || sec.id const val = resolveContent(base, key) if (val) { const parts = key.split('.') const shortKey = parts.length > 1 ? parts[parts.length-1] : parts[0] pc[shortKey] = val pc[key] = val } } return pc } ``` Then each section component receives `pageContent` and accesses its data by short key (e.g. `pageContent.hero`, `pageContent.programs`). ### Brand tokens (CSS custom properties) Always apply brand colors as inline styles or CSS variables — Tailwind `@theme` directives in `globals.css` are ignored by Pages Router: ```css /* src/pages/globals.css */ @import "tailwindcss"; @theme { --color-primary: #1B2A4A; --color-secondary: #C9A96E; --color-accent: #C9A96E; --color-background: #FFFFFF; --color-surface: #FFFFFF; --color-surface-alt: #F5F5F0; --color-text: #1B2A4A; --color-text-muted: #5C6B7A; --color-border: #E0E0E0; --color-success: #10b981; } ``` Then in components, reference inline: `#1B2A4A` for navy, `#C9A96E` for gold, `#F5F5F0` for light backgrounds. ### SEO per page For pages, load SEO data from `content.es.json` at the matching page key path: ```tsx const seo = resolveContent(content, `${pageId}Page.seo`) || resolveContent(content, `${pageId}.seo`) || pageConfig?.seoTitle || {} ``` Set `` and `<meta name="description">` from this data. ### Content shape reference for section components The `content/es.json` in this project has this nesting: ```json { "siteName": "...", "tagline": "...", "navigation": { "navItems": [...], "ctaText": "...", "ctaHref": "..." }, "home": { "hero": { "headline": "...", "subheadline": "...", "ctaPrimaryText": "...", "ctaPrimaryHref": "...", "ctaSecondaryText": "...", "trustBadges": [...] }, "stats": { "items": [{ "value": "...", "label": "...", "icon": "..." }] }, "trust": { "eyebrow": "...", "title": "...", "items": [{ "icon": "...", "title": "...", "description": "...", "image": "..." }] }, "programs": { "eyebrow": "...", "title": "...", "tiers": [{ "id": "...", "name": "...", "price": "...", "included": [...], "highlighted": bool }] }, "services": { "eyebrow": "...", "title": "bullets": [...] }] }, "process": { "eyebrow": "...", "title": "...", "steps": [{ "number": int, "title": "...", "description": "...", "duration": "..." }] }, "testimonials": { "eyebrow": "...",
